english: Double Unagi Rice Bowl with Miso Soup

food_subgroup: Rice bowl set meal with grilled eel, egg, and miso soup

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Rice dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for unagi don or Japanese eel rice bowl in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.

serving.description: 1 combo set (double unagi rice bowl + half boiled egg + miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately 650 g, comprising a double portion of grilled unagi over steamed rice (approximately 500 g), one half boiled egg (approximately 50 g), and a bowl of miso soup (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Unagi (eel) rice bowl (unadon) — component-based estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled freshwater eel, cooked Japanese short-grain rice, soy-based tare sauce, boiled egg, and miso soup
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods and SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a double unagi rice bowl combo as a composite dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: grilled freshwater eel (FDC ID 175168, SR Legacy), cooked short-grain white rice (FDC ID 169756, SR Legacy), soy-based tare glaze (estimated from soy sauce and sugar components), hard-boiled egg (FDC ID 173424, SR Legacy), and miso soup (FDC ID 172457, SR Legacy). The double-portion unagi bowl is modelled as approximately 500 g rice+eel+tare component and 50 g egg and 100 g miso soup, yielding a 650 g total serving. Per-100 g values are derived from this weighted composite. Values are estimates and should be verified against restaurant-specific nutritional disclosure when available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (Japanese short-grain, cooked) as primary GI driver; composite dish GI estimated by carbohydrate-weighted method
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a double unagi rice bowl combo. The GI is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted composite method. Cooked Japanese short-grain white rice has a GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). This rice component dominates the glycemic response of the dish (approximately 85% of total carbohydrate). Minor contributions from tare sauce (high-GI sugars) and miso soup (negligible carbohydrate) do not materially alter the composite GI. Composite GI is therefore estimated at 72 (high). Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 146.25) / 100 ≈ 105.3, rounded to 105.5. Carbohydrate per serving = 22.5 g/100g × 650 g = 146.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from tare (soy-based) glaze and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its high sodium content (approximately 310 mg per 100 g, equating to roughly 2015 mg per serving), which substantially approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients; requesting reduced tare sauce and omitting miso soup can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load driven by large double-portion of white rice and sweet tare glaze
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should exercise caution with this dish as the double-portion white rice base and sweet soy tare glaze produce a very high glycemic load per serving (approximately 105 g GL), which is likely to cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction, substitution with brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables are recommended strategies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from unagi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While the high sodium content (approximately 2015 mg per serving) is a concern for cardiovascular health, grilled freshwater eel provides beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) and vitamin D, which may partially offset cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and request reduced tare sauce to manage sodium and sugar intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Double Unagi Rice Bowl Combo (ダブルうな丼コンボ, Daburu Unadon Konbo) is a Japanese set meal consisting of a double portion of grilled freshwater eel (unagi) served over a generous bed of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, glazed with a sweet soy-based tare sauce, accompanied by a half boiled egg and a bowl of miso soup. Unagi don (unadon) is a classic Japanese dish with deep cultural roots, traditionally prepared by grilling the eel over charcoal using the kabayaki technique — the eel is butterflied, skewered, grilled, dipped repeatedly in tare sauce, and grilled again to produce a lacquered, caramelised exterior with a tender, rich interior. The double portion format doubles the eel topping relative to a standard unadon, making this a high-protein, energy-dense meal. The flavour profile is savoury-sweet with umami depth from the tare glaze, complemented by the mild richness of the eel and the neutral starchiness of the rice. Nutritionally, this combo is a significant source of protein (approximately 51 g per serving), vitamin B12, vitamin D, omega-3 fatty acids, and iron, contributed primarily by the eel. However, the large white rice base produces a very high glycemic load, and the tare sauce and miso soup contribute substantial sodium (approximately 2015 mg per serving). This dish is not suitable for individuals requiring strict glycemic control or sodium restriction without modification. In Singapore, this dish is served at Japanese casual dining chains such as Sukiya and is popular as a satisfying lunch or dinner option. The combo format with miso soup and egg adds nutritional variety and increases satiety.
common_user_queries: What is a double unagi bowl?
How many calories in a double unagi rice bowl combo?
Is unagi rice bowl good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a double unagi rice bowl combo as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for grilled freshwater eel, cooked short-grain white rice, soy-based tare glaze, boiled egg, and miso soup. The GI is estimated using the carbohydrate-weighted composite method anchored to the University of Sydney GI Database value for Japanese short-grain white rice. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and component sources are approved, but the composite has not been directly measured.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries. No direct laboratory analysis of this specific restaurant dish is available from an approved source. Values should be treated as indicative and verified against restaurant-specific nutritional disclosure when available. 2. The serving size of 650 g is an estimate based on typical double unadon portion weights reported in Japanese food composition literature; actual restaurant serving weights at Sukiya Singapore or Funan Mix & Match may differ. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for grilled eel and rice components — no industrial trans fats are present in the traditional preparation. 4. Glycemic load per serving is very high (approximately 105.5 g GL) due to the large double-portion white rice base; this is a clinically significant consideration for glycemic management. 5. Sodium per serving is approximately 2015 mg, primarily from tare sauce and miso soup; this is a clinically significant consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not individually confirmed by a single composite-dish measurement. 7. The food_id GD000214 is preserved from the input seed record. 8. Halal status is not confirmed; traditional unagi preparation may involve mirin (rice wine), which is not halal-certified; is_halal is set to false pending restaurant-specific confirmation.
